steps.prompt: Which word is the main verb?

hints.mihi: 'mihi' is a pronoun in the dative meaning 'to/for me'
hints.tempestatem: 'tempestatem' is a noun in the accusative meaning 'storm'

steps.prompt: What does 'tanti' express in this construction?
steps.target: Genitive of value — 'worth so much / worthwhile'
choices: Genitive of value — 'worth so much / worthwhile'

hints.Nominative plural: 'tanti' as nominative plural would modify a plural noun — here it's a genitive of value with 'est'
hints.Dative singular: Dative would be 'tanto' — 'tanti' is genitive of indefinite value
hints.Ablative of price: Ablative of price uses definite amounts — 'tanti' as genitive expresses indefinite value ('worth that much')

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'invidia' — 'invidiae' with -ae ending is genitive singular
hints.Dative: Dative singular is also 'invidiae', but here it modifies 'tempestatem' as a genitive (storm of resentment)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'tempestas' — 'tempestatem' with -em ending is accusative, object of 'subire'

hints.1st: 1st declension accusative ends in -am — 'tempestas, -atis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension accusative ends in -um — 'tempestas, -atis' is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension accusative ends in -em too, but 'tempestas, -atis' with gen. -atis is 3rd

hints.to approach: 'to approach' is close — 'subire' can mean 'approach' but here means 'undergo/endure'

hints.eo, ire, ii, itum: These are the parts of 'ire' (to go) — the base verb
hints.adeo, adire, adii, aditum: These are the parts of 'adire' (to approach) — a different compound
hints.pereo, perire, perii, peritum: These are the parts of 'perire' (to perish) — a different compound

steps.target: Political hostility as a storm to be weathered
choices: Actual bad weather during the speech
Political hostility as a storm to be weathered

hints.Actual bad weather during the speech: 'tempestas' is metaphorical here — Cicero compares political resentment to a storm
hints.A religious omen: While 'tempestas' can relate to omens, here it metaphorically describes political backlash
hints.A military siege: The metaphor is of a storm, not a siege — Cicero will endure the storm of resentment

translation: It is worth so much to me, Citizens, to undergo this storm of resentment.
